title: Cat Swollen Paw After Jumping: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your cat has a swollen paw after a jump. Learn about urgency levels, potential injuries, and how to help your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A swollen paw after jumping is typically a medium-priority injury requiring a veterinary exam within 12–24 hours, though immediate emergency care is necessary if the cat is non-weight bearing or has a visible deformity.

Why is my cat's paw swollen after jumping?

When a cat jumps and lands poorly, the force of the impact can cause various injuries to the delicate structures of the paw. Swelling is typically an inflammatory response to trauma, which could range from soft tissue bruising and sprained ligaments to more serious issues like a hairline fracture or a dislocated joint. It is your cat's body trying to protect the injured area by increasing blood flow and white blood cells to the site.

When is a swollen paw considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 12 to 24 hours for general swelling and significant discomfort.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your cat is completely non-weight bearing.
  • Visit an emergency clinic if there is a visible deformity or if the paw feels cold to the touch, as these can be signs of a complete break or nerve damage.

How can taking a photo of my cat's paw help the triage process?

  • Providing a clear, well-lit photo allows the veterinary team to assess symmetry by comparing the swollen paw to a healthy one.
  • A visual allows professionals to look for skin discoloration, puncture wounds, or broken claws that occurred during the landing.
  • This information helps determine the severity of the injury and how quickly your cat needs to be examined in person.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following a jump or fall, a cat presenting with a swollen paw should be assessed for palmar carpal breakdown, characterized by lameness, carpal swelling, and a plantigrade stance due to tearing of the palmar carpal ligaments. Clinical signs of joint fractures, such as lameness, pain, and joint swelling, should also be considered. Radiography and CT are useful in delineating fractures and assessing the extent of the injury. It is important to minimize movement of the head, neck, and spine during transport to the hospital; a flat, firm board can be used to provide support.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human pain medications like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol, as these are highly toxic to felines and can be fatal.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: The best thing you can do is restrict their movement. Keep your cat in a carrier or a small room without furniture they can jump on to prevent further injury.

faqs.question: What are the signs that the injury is serious?
faqs.answer: Serious signs include the bone protruding through the skin, the paw hanging at an unnatural angle, extreme vocalization, or the cat hiding and refusing to eat.
